coredata.dc:title: Quantitative detection of naphthenic acids in wastewater based on superior fluorescence performance of nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yize
Li, Meiyu
Zhang, Ruoyao
Zhao, Tengda
Han, Jing
Kang, Aibin
Chen, Chunmao
Ren, Hongwei
Duan, Erhong
coredata.dc:description: With the development of the petrochemical industry, a large amount of naphthenic acids in petrochemical wastewater was accumulated in the environment, causing serious environmental pollution. Most of the commonly used methods for the determination of naphthenic acids have the characteristics of high energy consumption, complicated pretreatment, long detection cycle, and the need to send samples to analytical laboratories. Therefore, it is essential to develop an efficient and low-cost field analytical method for rapidly naphthenic acids quantify. In this study, nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) based on natural deep eutectic solvents (NADESs) was successfully synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method. The fluorescence property of carbon quantum dots was used to achieve the quantitative detection of naphthenic acids in wastewater. The prepared N-CQDs showed excellent fluorescence and stability, showed a good response to naphthenic acids and a linear relationship in the concentration range of naphthenic acids from 0.03 to 0.09 mol‧L−1. The effect of common interferents in petrochemical wastewater on the detection of naphthenic acids by N-CQDs was investigated. The results showed that N-CQDs had good specificity for the detection of naphthenic acids. N-CQDs was applied to the naphthenic acids wastewater, and the concentration of naphthenic acids in the wastewater was successfully calculated according to the fitting equation.

head.ce:title: Quantitative detection of naphthenic acids in wastewater based on superior fluorescence performance of nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the development of the petrochemical industry, a large amount of naphthenic acids in petrochemical wastewater was accumulated in the environment, causing serious environmental pollution. Most of the commonly used methods for the determination of naphthenic acids have the characteristics of high energy consumption, complicated pretreatment, long detection cycle, and the need to send samples to analytical laboratories. Therefore, it is essential to develop an efficient and low-cost field analytical method for rapidly naphthenic acids quantify. In this study, nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) based on natural deep eutectic solvents (NADESs) was successfully synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method. The fluorescence property of carbon quantum dots was used to achieve the quantitative detection of naphthenic acids in wastewater. The prepared N-CQDs showed excellent fluorescence and stability, showed a good response to naphthenic acids and a linear relationship in the concentration range of naphthenic acids from 0.03 to 0.09 mol‧L−1. The effect of common interferents in petrochemical wastewater on the detection of naphthenic acids by N-CQDs was investigated. The results showed that N-CQDs had good specificity for the detection of naphthenic acids. N-CQDs was applied to the naphthenic acids wastewater, and the concentration of naphthenic acids in the wastewater was successfully calculated according to the fitting equation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Petroleum acids account for about 1–4 % of petroleum, including naphthenic acids (NAs), fatty acids, aromatic acids, and other substances (inorganic acids, phenols, mercaptan, etc.). Of these, the proportion of NAs is extremely high, usually around 90 % (Clemente and Fedorak, 2005; Headley and McMartin, 2004). With the development of the petrochemical industry, a large amount of NAs in petrochemical wastewater was accumulated in the environment, causing serious environmental pollution. NAs have many properties such as corrosive, surface active, difficult to volatilize, and difficult to degrade. It was classified as an emerging pollutant (Abdalrhman et al., 2021) which is extremely difficult to treat. Besides, NAs usually represents a class of substances. There are many kinds of NAs, and its composition is more complex than common pollutions. All of these make higher requirements for the characteristics detection. The most commonly used methods for the analysis of NAs include Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, ion chromatography, gas-mass spectrometry, electrospray ionization mass spectrometry and high-performance liquid chromatography (Colati et al., 2013; Headley et al., 2016; Lu et al., 2013; Mohamed et al., 2008; Remolina et al., 2022). However, these methods suffer from high energy consumption, complicated pretreatment (e.g., tedious and time-consuming process of derivatization of carboxyl groups in NAs using derivatizing agents), long assay cycle time, and the need to send the samples to the analytical laboratory (Ripmeester and Duford, 2019). Therefore, it is essential to develop an efficient and low-cost analytical method for real-time NAs quantitative detection, which would be great helpful in practical application.

Fluorescence spectroscopy based on fluorescent probes has been recognized as an efficient analytical detection method due to its simple method, good selectivity, high sensitivity and low price (Jayan et al., 2021; Plácido et al., 2019; Salahinejad et al., 2021; Singh et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2006; Zhu et al., 2021). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a new type of carbon-based zero-dimensional nanomaterial developed on the basis of traditional quantum dots. Compared with semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs has the advantages of low cost, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, simple preparation method, easy surface functionalization, and excellent optical properties. Therefore, CQDs has a wide range of applications in fluorescent sensors (Yang et al., 2021a,b,c), photocatalysis (Liu et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2016), electrochemistry (Xu et al., 2014), and biosensing (Molaei, 2019a, 2019b). However, it was found that common CQDs has deficiencies in catalytic activity due to the insufficient active sites. Thanks to the passivation effect of the surface nitrogen atoms, the nitrogen-rich CQDs (N-CQDs) exhibited more excellent optoelectronic properties, which largely compensate for the above-mentioned defect (Du and Guo, 2016). An increasing number of researchers had proposed that nitrogen-containing groups can inhibit the complexation of nonradiative electron-hole pairs, leading to high reactivity of N-CQDs (Ren et al., 2022). Therefore, N-CQDs exhibit excellent optoelectronic properties and have considerable potential for the fluorescence detection of NAs.

Improving the structure and properties of CQDs by optimizing the composition of precursors is an effective way to design CQDs. Now, in the selection of CQDs precursors, researchers are increasingly inclined to choose biological materials from nature (Durian (Wang et al., 2018); Azarditcha indica (Yadav et al., 2018); Polyalthia longifolia (Kolaprath et al., 2022); Morus alba L. (Zhou et al., 2022), etc.) or their derivatives as source (Beer (Li et al., 2022), etc.). Such kind of green precursors often could obtain relatively high QY. However, most of the biomaterials in nature have complex structure. In addition, with the changing of environmental factors, the content of certain elements in biomass may change greatly. It would produce significant uncertainties for the preparation and structural design of CQDs. As a class of biocompatible polymers, polyethylene glycol has many excellent properties, such as low cost, low viscosity, low toxicity and high boiling point. These advantages make polyethylene glycol often selected as the HBD to prepare DES, and then to synthesize CQDs. CQDs derived from polyethylene glycol is intrinsically less toxic, and with better biocompatibility (Peng et al., 2020). Meanwhile，the structure of polyethylene glycol is determined and known, so the structure of CQDs can be initially designed before preparation.

This work designed an N-CQDs using polyethylene glycol as a base precursor and investigated its application in the petrochemical industry. In this study, a green spectroscopic method (fluorescence) is introduced into the petrochemical industry to evaluate the condition of NAs pollution. N-CQDs based on green-designable precursor of natural deep eutectic solvents (NADESs) was successfully synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method. The fluorescence property of N-CQDs was used to achieve the quantitative detection of NAs. The structural features of the synthesized N-CQDs were evaluated and the fluorescence properties of the N-CQDs were discussed in detail. In addition, the detection performance of NAs by N-CQDs was investigated. The effects of common interfering substances in petrochemical wastewater and the environmental tolerance of N-CQDs were discussed. The synthesized N-CQDs was successfully applied to the quantitative detection of NAs in wastewater.

5-Amino-tetrazole-monohydrate, polyethylene glycol 400, phenol and acetone were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous ethanol and anhydrous methanol were obtained from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Naphthenic acids (3-Cyclohexenecarboxylic), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Concentrated hydrochloric acid was obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). The wastewater samples of naphthenic acids were taken from the electro-desalting wastewater of a refinery wastewater treatment plant in Qinhuangdao, Hebei Province, China(NAs wastewater-QHD), wastewater from Liaohe Petrochemical Company in Liaoning Province, China(NAs wastewater-LN) and wastewater from an oil refinery in Shijiazhuang, Hebei province, China(NAs wastewater-SJZ). All reagents were used as received without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken using an F20 TEM (JEM-2100, Hitachi, Japan), with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Energy disperse spectroscopy (EDS) and mapping images were collected using an energy dispersive spectrometer (Aztec Xmax 50, Oxford). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained using an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with an Al K Alpha (hv = 1340 eV) in standard lens mode (Thermo Fisher, Waltham, USA). Nuclear magnetic spectra (1H NMR, 13C NMR) were recorded using a nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (Bruker, Avance 500 MHz, Germany) using DMSO as the solvent. The model of instrument used for solid NMR is BRUKER AVANCE NEO 400WB. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained via the KBr pellet method using a Bruker Tensor 27 (Germany) spectrophotometer with wavenumbers from 4000 to 400 cm−1. Ultraviolet and visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-6100S UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Metash instrument Co., Ltd., China) with a 1.0-cm quartz cuvette. Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with a 150 W lamp as the excitation source. Time-resolved luminescence decays and the absolute fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) were acquired using an Edinburgh FLS 980 spectrometer. The model of instrument used for Raman spectrum HORIBA Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CQDs based on NADESs
ce:section.ce:para: NADESs was prepared using 5-Amino-tetrazole-monohydrate (5-ATZ) and polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG400) as precursors. 3 g 5-ATZ and 11.64 g PEG400 were mixed in a 250 mL three-necked flask at the molar ratios of 1:1. The mixture was heated and stirred at 110 °C for 2.5 h until the homogeneous and transparent stable liquid was formed as NADESs. N-CQDs based on NADESs was prepared using a one-step solvothermal method. 1 g of NADESs and 30 mL of anhydrous ethanol were added to 50 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at 230 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the N-CQDs solutions prepared by hydrothermal method were firstly put into ten 10 mL centrifuge tubes after standing for 1 h. After centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min, 1 mL solutions at the bottom of centrifuge tubes were left, and the rest of solutions were transferred to the new centrifuge tubes for further centrifugation. After three repetitions, the remaining solution was collected. Then the residual solvent of ethanol in the solution was removed by rotary evaporation. Last but not the least, the left solution was filtered three times using 0.45 μm and 0.22 μm organic filter membrane, respectively. Finally, a small amount of deionized water was added to the solution and mixed well, and the mixture was freeze-dried for 72 h to obtain the solid product.

To evaluate the influence of temperature on the fluorescence stability, 10 mL N-CQDs solution was added to 10 mL centrifuge tubes and maintained at 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, and 60 °C for 1 h, respectively. The solution was transferred to the cuvette quickly and analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The effect of storage time was detected after the N-CQDs solution was kept at 25 °C for 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25 days later.

A solution with varying pH (1–14) was prepared using sodium hydroxide and 37 % concentrated hydrochloric acid. To determine the influence of pH on the fluorescence stability, 4 mL N-CQDs and 1 mL prepared pH solution from 1 to 14 were added to 10 mL centrifuge tubes, respectively. Subsequently, the mixtures were mixed ultrasonically. The fluorescence spectra was detected by sequence at an excitation wavelength of 355 nm.

The common pollutants in petrochemical wastewater include alcohols, aldehydes, ketones, phenols and benzenes, and other complex organic compounds. Therefore, methanol, phenol, and acetone, which are more representative, were chosen as interferents in the experiment. By comparing the effects of different interferents on the fluorescence performance of N-CQDs, the selectivity of N-CQDs for the detection of NAs recognition under complex environmental conditions of petroleum wastewater pollutants was investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a system for the detection of NAs by N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A series of standard solutions of model NAs with a concentration gradient (0.03–0.8 mol‧L−1) were configured for the experiment. N-CQDs and different concentrations of model NAs solutions were mixed well within 5 s and then tested for fluorescence emission spectra. The fluorescence quenching rate was calculated based on the tested fluorescence intensity (Define “(F 0-F)/F 0” as the fluorescence quenching rate. “F 0” is the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs at 355 nm excitation wavelength, “F” is the fluorescence intensity after adding different concentrations of NAs solution, and the emission wavelength range is 350–550 nm in 355 nm excitation wavelength). With the fluorescence quenching rate as the vertical coordinate and the concentration of NAs solution as the horizontal coordinate, the linear and nonlinear standard curves were fitted to construct a system for the detection of NAs by N-CQDs.
On this basis, the experiment also explored the specificity of N-CQDs for the detection of NAs in a mixed system of NAs with each of the three interfering substances.
To prevent the concentration of NAs in wastewater is too high, wastewater was diluted 0.5 times with deionized water and then mixed with N-CQDs, tested for fluorescence emission spectra.

In addition, the tolerance of the detection system to ambient temperature and pH was also investigated. Considering the complexity of the actual testing environment of wastewater sample, the environmental tolerance of N-CQDs for the detection of NAs wastewater was investigated under the temperature conditions from 0 °C to 60 °C and pH from 1 to 14.

N-CQDs was synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image is shown in Fig. 1(a). The obtained N-CQDs is spherical and distributed evenly. The particle size distribution (inserted in Fig. 1(a)) shows that the N-CQDs is 16–30 nm in size with an average diameter of 37.8 nm. The N-CQDs with different precursor mole ratios (1:0.75, 1:2) are shown in Fig. S1. The N-CQDs was not successfully produced when the preparation mole ratio was 1:0.75. When the mole ratio was 1:2, the carbide particle size obtained by hydrothermal method ranged from 200 to 700 nm, and N-CQDs was not successfully synthesized. Therefore, the precursor mole ratio of 1:1 was chosen as the optimized composition for the N-CQDs preparation.

To verify that nitrogen elements were doped successfully, EDS and mapping were used to analyze the types of elements and the atomic proportion of N-CQDs (Fig. 1(b-e)). The mapping results show that the carbon (Fig. 1(c)), nitrogen (Fig. 1(d)) and oxygen (Fig. 1(e)) elements were all uniformly distributed on the surface of N-CQDs. It shows the successful synthesis of N-CQDs. The concentration of three elements and atomic percentage are shown in Table S1. It could be found that carbon, nitrogen and oxygen atoms account for 52.6 %, 13.7 % and 33.7 %, respectively. This result indicated that nitrogen element is highly doped into N-CQDs.

Moreover, XPS was carried out to explore the chemical bonding between the elements. Fig. 2(a) shows a wide full scan XPS spectrum with distinct peaks at 284.0 eV, 398.3 eV and 531.1 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 2(b) shows two peaks with binding energies at 284.0 eV and 285.8 eV, which represent the CC/CC and sp2 carbon, CN bonds, respectively. The N1s spectrum in Fig. 2(c) was fitted as a peak with a binding energy of 398.0 eV, which is attributed to the CNC bond. However, due to the much lower content of CNC, the characteristic intensity of CNC peak is weaker compared to C and O elements, and the baseline oscillation of the equipment leads to a not very smooth peak pattern. Moreover, the N-CQDs in Fig. 2(d) peak at 531.0 eV represents the CO bond. To sum up, the presence of nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs further confirms the successful doping of nitrogen atoms. The XPS results confirm the abundance of functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs.

1H NMR (Fig. 2(e)) and 13C NMR (Fig. 2(f)) spectra of the N-CQDs served as an assistant to characterize the types of carbon and hydrogen in the N-CQDs. In Fig. 2(e), the triplet at 1.05 ppm and the split peak at 3.41–3.46 ppm of N-CQDs represent the characteristic hydrogen atoms in CH; the split peak at 4.34–4.36 ppm is ascribed to the characteristic hydrogen atoms in NH2. Fig. 2(f) shows that the 13C NMR spectrum is mainly divided into one peak and a small peak cluster. The peak of 17.1 ppm is caused by the C in R2CH2. The peak clusters at 57.3 ppm, 60.6 ppm, and 69.7 ppm represent the absorption of carbon atoms in CN, COH and COC. The results were consistent with that of solid NMR spectra of N-CQDs (Fig. S2).

FT-IR spectra was employed to determine the surface functional groups of N-CQDs. Fig. 3 shows the FT-IR spectra of 5-ATZ, PEG400, NADESs and N-CQDs, respectively. FT-IR spectra of 5-ATZ are present with the stretching vibrations of CN, NN, and CN bonds, the stretching vibration and asymmetric bending vibration of NH2. The FT-IR spectra of PEG400 shows the presence of stretching vibrations of CO, CH, and OH bonds and the bending vibrations of CH bonds in methylene groups. The FT-IR spectral results of the two precursors are fully consistent with the structural features of both. In the FT-IR spectra of NADESs, it can be seen that the weak absorption double peaks at 841 cm−1 and 933 cm−1 represent the bending vibration of the CH bond. The strong absorption peak at 1100 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of the CO bond, and the peak at 2900 cm−1 represents the bending vibration of the CH bond in methylene. These characteristic peaks did not change with respect to the precursor. The peak at 1640 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the CN bond, and the peak intensity is weakened relative to the precursor. The absorption peaks at 3240 cm−1 and 3360 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of the OH in the hydroxyl group and the NH in the amino group. There were no new chemical bonds formed during the formation of NADESs. The partial enhancement and weakening of the chemical bonds indicated that hydrogen bonds were formed between 5-ATZ and PEG400, they were combined by hydrogen bonds to form NADESs without chemical reactions.

Regarding the FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs, the absorption peak at 656 cm−1 represents the out-of-plane bending vibration of -NH2 in the amide group, the absorption peak at 879 cm−1 represents the bending vibration of the CH bond in the carbon‑carbon double bond, and the absorption double peaks at 1050 cm−1 and 1090 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of the CO bond and the stretching vibration of the COC bond in the carboxyl group. In addition, the absorption peak at 1400 cm−1 may represent the stretching vibration of NN and CC bonds, and the bending vibration of NH bonds. During the synthesis of N-CQDs from NADESs by high temperature solvothermal reactions, a part of the groups changed, with increased carbonation, more contracted groups, and partial conjugated structures. The FT-IR results prove that the surface of N-CQDs contains nitrogen-containing functional groups, which is consistent with the XPS characterization results.

The carbon structure of N-CQDs was confirmed by Raman spectra (Fig. 4 ). The broad peak at 1342 cm−1 is assigned to the D band, which corresponds to the sp3 defects in N-CQDs. The peak at 1572 cm−1 is assigned to the G band, which is a band related to the in-plane bond-stretching motion of C sp2 atoms, indicating that there are aromatic groups inside N-CQDs. The integrated intensities ratio of D to G bands (ID/IG) is calculated to 1.58. It suggests that the defective graphitic carbon phase of the CDs is formed in hydrothermal process (Sahu et al., 2012; Yang et al., 2011).

As shown in Fig. 5(a) (black line), the absorption band in the range of 250–320 nm may be associated with the π-π* transition of CC and the n-π* transition of CO; the absorption band in the 330–380 nm range may originate from surface defect states caused by surface functional groups of N-CQDs. (Liu et al., 2022; Lu et al., 2017; Miao et al., 2018; Song et al., 2022).

The blue and red lines in Fig. 5(a) represent the fluorescence excitation spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs, respectively. The excitation spectrum extends from 300 nm to 400 nm with only one peak located at 355 nm. Under excitation at 355 nm, the N-CQDs emitted bright blue fluorescence (Fig. 5(a) inset). In the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 400 nm (Fig. 5(b)), the maximum emission wavelength of the fluorescence emission spectrum occurs red-shifted with the increasing excitation wavelength, the intensity of emission peak increases first and then decreases with the redshift of emission peak, which indicates a clear excitation dependence of N-CQDs (Lin et al., 2021). The excitation-dependent PL behavior were similar to the common carbon quantum dots, which was most likely caused by the complex carbon cores or surface defects (Zhu et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2013; Fang et al., 2019). Besides, the fluorescence emission wavelength is always greater than the excitation light wavelength, so the fluorescence properties of N-CQDs are in accordance with the Stokes shift law. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs is 5.82 ns (Fig. S3). The fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of N-CQDs was 7.22 % at 375 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. S4).

To investigate the effect of temperature, the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs were measured at 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 °C. Fig. 5(c) illustrates the fluorescence intensities for N-CQDs at different temperatures. The change of temperature didn't result in an obvious variation in the fluorescence intensity except for a slight reduction at 0 °C. Overall, the fluorescence intensity remains stable, indicating that the N-CQDs have high-intensity fluorescence stability, and can be used in a wide range of temperature environment.

The stability of fluorescence performance of N-CQDs under the storage time of 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25 and 30 days were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5(d), the prepared N-CQDs had good time stability and can be stored for more than 30 days.

To explore the effect of pH on N-CQDs, the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs in various pH values from 1 to 14 were listed in Fig. 5(e). The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs showed slight changes under the conditions of pH 2 to 12 range. However, the fluorescence intensity decreased when pH was 1, 13 and 14. It means that the suitable working pH condition of N-CQDs ranged from 2 to 12, less not suitable under strong acid and base conditions. The test results showed that N-CQDs had good pH stability over a wide pH range (pH = 2–12) and can be used in most testing environments. It provides a convenient way for wastewater detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of model NA by N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the price of NAs on the market and to make the work more typical, 3-Cyclohexanecarboxylic was chosen as the model NAs. Because it is not only cheaper but also has a relatively more complex structure (both an olefin structure and contains a carbon ring and a carboxyl group). On this basis, different concentrations of NAs solutions were configured, and the detection performance of N-CQDs on NAs was investigated. The fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs was investigated after different concentration (0.03–0.8 mol‧L−1) of 3-Cyclohexenecarboxylic was added to the system (Fig. 6(a)). The fluorescence quenching rate of N-CQDs was 32.70 % when the NAs concentration was 0.03 mol‧L−1 NAs solution. With the increasing of the NAs concentration, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs had a regular and obvious gradual decrease. The fluorescence quenching rate reached 96.41 % at the NAs concentration of 0.4 mol‧L−1. As the concentration of NAs continued to increase, the trend of the N-CQDs fluorescence intensity gradually became slower. Finally, when the NAs concentration was 0.8 mol‧L−1, the fluorescence of N-CQDs was almost completely quenched, and the fluorescence quenching rate was close up to 100 %.

The results of fitting the nonlinear relationship between different concentrations of NAs on the fluorescence quenching rate of N-CQDs were shown in Fig. 6(b). It was found that, the increasing trend of fluorescence quenching rate was obvious in the range of 0.03–0.3 mol‧L−1 of NAs concentration. The fluorescence quenching rate did not change much in the range of 0.4–0.8 mol‧L−1 of NAs concentration. The fit between the fluorescence quenching rate of N-CQDs and the concentration of NAs in the range of 0.03–0.8 mol‧L−1 was consistent with a nonlinear relationship, (F 0-F)/F 0 = 96.949–58.31/(1 + (x/0.101)^6.025), R2 = 0.984.

As shown in Fig. 6(c), the fluorescence quenching rate of N-CQDs and the concentration of NAs conform to the nonlinear fitting relationship in the range of 0.03–0.4 mol·L−1. The fitted formula is (F 0-F)/F 0 = 97.119–70.40/(1 + (x/0.087)^2.518), R2 = 0.995. As shown in Fig. 6(d), the linear relationship was found in the range of 0.03–0.09 mol‧L−1 of NAs concentration, and the fitted result was (F 0-F)/F 0 = 350.65x + 22.06, R2 = 0.971. The LOD of N-CQDs was calculated. As shown in Table 1 , the fitted equation (F 0-F)/F 0 = 350.65x + 22.06 for 0.03–0.09 mol·L−1 was chosen, using a 0.05 mol·L−1 solution of NAs. The limit of detection for N-CQDs was calculated as 0.000588 mol·L−1 (74 mg·L−1) using the equation LOD = 3σ/s (Yang et al., 2021b; Yue et al., 2019). Where “σ” is the standard deviation (n = 5) and “s” is the slope of the linear fit equation (σ = 0.0687, s = 350.65). In summary, the fluorescence quenching rate of N-CQDs and the concentration of NAs have a good linear or nonlinear relationship under different concentration range of NAs. A system for quantitative detection NAs in wastewater was successfully constructed.

Fig. S5 showed images of N-CQDs response to different concentration (0.03–0.8 mol‧L−1) of model NAs under UV light of 365 nm. After the addition of model NAs solution, the blue fluorescence of N-CQDs gradually darkened with the increasing of NAs solution concentration.

Fig. 7(a) and (b) showed the FL spectra fluorescence and quenching rate (F 0-F)/F 0 of N-CQDs mixed with phenol, acetone, methanol and model NAs solutions, respectively. Under the same concentration conditions, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was basically similar after the addition of phenol, acetone and methanol solutions, while the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly after the addition of NAs solution. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the fluorescence quenching rates of 1 mol‧L−1 phenol, acetone, methanol and NAs for N-CQDs were 49.51 %, 46.71 %, 52.5 % and 69.04 %, respectively. These indicate that only NAs has the best quenching effect on N-CQDs among the four substances, and N-CQDs have good selectivity for the recognition detection of NAs.

The detection ability of N-CQDs was investigated in the coexistence of NAs with phenol (Fig. 7(c)), acetone (Fig. 7(e)) and methanol (Fig. 7(g)). The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs gradually decreased with the increasing of NAs concentration in the presence of interfering substances, and the decrease trend was almost the same as that of the fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDs acting on NAs alone. The fitted results of fluorescence quenching rate of N-CQDs and NAs concentration under the coexistence of three kinds of interferers and NAs all conform to nonlinear relationship: ① Phenol: (F 0-F)/F 0 = 123.266–99.33/(1 + (x/0.159)^1.119)， R2 = 0.994 (Fig. 6(d)); ② Acetone:(F 0-F)/F 0 = 101.707–64.60/(1 + (x/1.135)^2.386)， R2 = 0.995 (Fig. 6(f)); ③ Methanol: (F 0-F)/F 0 = 104.076–70.33/(1 + (x/0.109)^1.670)， R2 = 0.998 (Fig. 6(h)). Overall, none of the three interferents had a significant effect on the system for the detection of NAs by N-CQDs under coexistence conditions.

Fig. S6, S7 and S8 showed the dark box UV images of N-CQDs, N-CQDs mixed with different concentrations of NAs solutions and certain concentrations of interferent solution under 365 nm UV irradiation (from left to right). It could be seen that the blue fluorescence of N-CQDs was gradually dimmed with the increasing concentration of NAs solution.

In addition to 3-Cyclohexenecarboxylic acid, Cyclohexanecarboxylic acid, Benzoic acid and 3-Cyclohexanepropionic acid were selected to further investigate the detectability of N-CQDs (Yang et al., 2014). The experimental results are shown in Fig. S9. It can be seen that the N-CQDs also showed good detection performance for Cyclohexanecarboxylic acid, Benzoic acid and 3-Cyclohexanepropionic acid.

On the basis of the above experiments, the NAs detection experiment of CQDs from literatures were also carried out (Fig.S10). Three different carbon quantum dots were prepared according to references: CQDs① (Zhu et al., 2013), CQDs② (Gao et al., 2019) and CQDs③ (Fan et al., 2014). It can be seen from Fig. S10(a) that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs① becomes stronger after adding NAs solutions. From Fig. S11(b), it can be seen that the model NAs solution itself has certain fluorescence and its fluorescence center basically overlaps with CQDs①, the enhanced fluorescence intensity of CQDs① mixed with NAs may originate from the fluorescence of the NAs solution itself. So for CQDs①, NAs cannot be detected by fluorescence quenching method. The fluorescence intensity of CQDs② was reduced after the addition of 0.1 moL·L−1 and 0.3 moL·L−1 NAs solutions, but the quenching effect was weaker than N-CQDs (Fig.S10(b)). As shown in Fig. S12, the absorption peak of CQDs② at 236 nm is associated with the π-π* transition of CC bond of aromatic sp2 domains from the carbon core, which represents the “core state” of CQDs②(Gao et al., 2019). After mixing with NAs solution, the absorption peak of CQDs② at 236 nm disappeared. It may be due to the quenching behavior of the “core state” of CQDs② (Yang et al., 2021b). CQDs③ did not exhibit quenching effect after the addition of NAs solutions(Fig.S10(c)). As shown in Fig. S13, there are no overlapping areas in the center of CQDs③ and model NAs solution fluorescence. It may be the reason why CQDs③ did not exhibit quenching effect after the addition of NAs solution.

The experiment also explored the applicability of N-CQDs in wastewater sample. The qualitative analysis of the wastewater sample was achieved by GCMS, and the test conditions were developed with reference to the study of Ye et al. (Ye et al., 2020). The total ion chromatogram (TIC, Fig. S14) and database search results (Table S2) of the wastewater sample are shown in Supporting Information.

Fig. 8(a) showed the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs, NAs wastewater-QHD, and N-CQDs in response to the diluted NAs wastewater-QHD. N-CQDs can emit bright blue light. It showed a very strong fluorescence effect at the excitation wavelength of 355 nm with the fluorescence intensity 7483.5 a.u. After the addition of NAs wastewater-QHD which did not have fluorescence effect (fluorescence intensity was 578.2 a.u.), N-CQDs underwent a significant fluorescence quenching effect with a significant decrease in brightness to the fluorescence intensity of 3546 a.u.

To further demonstrate the quenching effect, the change of blue fluorescence of N-CQDs before and after the addition of NAs wastewater-QHD was investigated (Fig. S15). It could be seen that the blue fluorescence of N-CQDs became significantly darker after the addition of NAs wastewater-QHD. The fluorescence quenching rate of N-CQDs was calculated to be 52.61 % for NAs wastewater-QHD. The NAs concentration was calculated to be 0.087 mol‧L−1 when the fluorescence quenching rate value was substituted into the linear relationship (F 0-F)/F 0 = 350.65x + 22.06. The concentration of NAs in NAs wastewater-QHD was thus deduced to be 0.1305 mol‧L−1.

According to the fluorescence quenching relationship (F 0-F)/F 0 = 350.65x + 22.06, the corresponding NAs concentration was calculated. The repeat tests were carried out for 3 times to calculated standard deviation and relative standard deviation (Table S3). The relative standard deviation (4.854 %) has proved the N-CQDs for NAs analysis merits great precision.

To evaluation the reliability of the N-CQDs detection method, the concentrations of wastewater sample of NAs were measured with reference to existing methods. The concentrations of NAs were 0.1125 mol‧L−1 (HPLC; Yen et al., 2004), 0.1179 mol‧L−1 (GC–MS; Scott et al., 2008) and 0.1253 mol‧L−1 (FTIR; Yen et al., 2004), respectively. The results of the four traditional methods confirmed the reliability of N-CQDs method.

The tolerance of the assay system to ambient temperature and pH was investigated. The fluorescence spectra of NAs wastewater-QHD acting on N-CQDs under different temperature conditions (Fig. 8(b)) showed that the highest fluorescence intensity of the mixed system was observed when the ambient temperature was 0 °C, and the lowest fluorescence intensity of the mixed system was observed when the ambient temperature was 60 °C. In the whole range from 0 to 60 °C, the fluorescence intensity of the mixed system of N-CQDs and NAs wastewater-QHD remained relatively stable with the increasing of temperature. This is supported by the trend point diagram (Fig. S16) of the fluorescence intensity change under different temperature conditions. In obvious contrast, as shown in Fig. 8(c), the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs decreased rapidly after the addition of NAs wastewater-QHD. As to the effect of environmental pH, the fluorescence intensity hardly changed in the full pH range (Fig. S17), and the fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs mixed with NAs wastewater-QHD were almost free from interference. In summary, the system for the NAs detection by N-CQDs is not greatly affected by ambient temperature and pH, and is environmentally tolerant.

Fig. 8(d, e, f) shows the 3D fluorescence plots of NAs wastewater-QHD (diluted 0.5 times), N-CQDs, and N-CQDs mixed with NAs wastewater-QHD, respectively. From Fig. 8(d), it is shown that the wastewater sample has a clear fluorescence center at (λ ex285 nm, λ em356 nm) and the fluorescence intensity is weak. Fig. 8(e) shows a clear fluorescence center of N-CQDs at (λ ex345 nm, λ em410 nm) and a strong fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. 8(f), the fluorescence center of the mixture of N-CQDs and the diluted 0.5-fold NAs solution is located at (λ ex305 nm, λ em395 nm) and the intensity is weakened relative to N-CQDs. These phenomena suggest that the fluorescence centers of N-CQDs is changed after the addition of wastewater sample, and the reason is most likely due to the chemical reaction of NAs with the functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs, which led to the change of the fluorescence properties of N-CQDs.

To further investigate the universality of N-CQDs detection performance for NAs in wastewater, N-CQDs was also applied to determine the NAs concentration of wastewater-LN and wastewater-SJZ. The relevant experimental data are shown in Fig. S18 and Fig. S19. The fluorescence quenching rate of NAs wastewater-LN and NAs wastewater-SJZ for N-CQDs were calculated based on the data in Fig. S18(d) and Fig. S19(d). By substituting the obtained results into the above linear equation, the concentrations of NAs in NAs wastewater-LN and NAs wastewater-SJZ were calculated to be 0.103 mol‧L−1 and 0.072 mol‧L−1, respectively. In conclusion, N-CQDs has good practical application performance for the detection of NAs in water.

To further explore the convenient application of N-CQDs, the cheap and easy-carrying degreasing cotton was used as a carrier for N-CQDs imitating pH indicator paper. The N-CQDs was uniformly dispersed on the degreasing cotton and then dried at 80 °C for 30 min to obtain the dried N-CQDs fluorescent degreasing cotton (FLDC). Based on the brightness variation, N-CQDs could quickly (usually within 5 s) produce fluorescent signal for different concentrations of NAs under the irradiation of the micro-portable UV lamp.

Fig. 9 showed the fluorescence luminescence of original degreasing cotton and FLDC under 365 nm UV light irradiation. It did not fluoresce under UV light, while FLDC could emit bright blue fluorescence. Fig. 10 showed the fluorescence luminescence of FLDC and the FLDC with different concentrations of NAs solution under 365 nm UV light irradiation. With the increasing of NAs concentration, the brightness of FLDC decreased gradually. It indicates that it is feasible to use degreasing cotton loaded with N-CQDs to make a material similar to “cotton swabs” for the convenient detection of NAs.

To better understand the performance of fluorescence detection method by N-CQDs, a comprehensive table is made. In the Table 2 , the advantages and disadvantages are compared with the common methods. In previous studies (de Araújo et al., 2022; Hindle et al., 2013; Scott et al., 2008; Shang et al., 2013), researchers mostly used chromatography, mass spectrometry, or a combination of the two methods to detect NAs quantitatively. Every method has its unique advantage and disadvantages that need to be improved. Only the “cooperation” of different methods will further acquire aspired detection performance of NAs. Due to the complexity of NAs, most of these traditional methods needs relatively complex pretreatment before detection, time-cost, and the samples cannot be recycled. The target method in this work merits the advantage of short detection response time, easy operation, and green precursor.

Furthermore, the photoluminescence quenching was analyzed using the Stern−Volmer model (Song et al., 2014; Yue et al., 2019):
(1)
F 0
/ F – 1 =
K SV
Q
=
k q
τ 0
Q
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of the N-CQDs in the absence and in the presence of NAs, respectively; K SV is the Stern−Volmer quenching constant; [Q] is the concentration of NAs; k q is the quenching rate constant, and τ 0 is the fluorescence lifetime of the N-CQDs.

As presented in Fig. 11(a), (F 0/F − 1) showed a linear correlation with the concentration of NAs between 0.03 and 0.09 M (F 0/F–1 = 11.95 [NAs] + 0.08), suggesting that the Stern−Volmer quenching constant K SV = 11.95 M−1. Taking τ 0 (5.82 ns) and K SV (11.95 M−1) into formula (1), k q is calculated to be 2.05 × 109 M−1 s−1, which is lower than the maximum value (1.0 × 1010) of dynamic quenching, verifying dynamic quenching is likely to happen in the detection system of this study (Yang et al., 2021b; Yue et al., 2019).

The fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs and N-CQDs-NAs mixed solution were tested. As shown in Fig. 11(b), the red line and black line represent the fluorescence lifetime curves of N-CQDs (5.82 ns) and N-CQDs-NAs (3.70 ns) mixed solution, respectively. After the addition of NAs, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs was shortened from 5.82 ns to 3.70 ns, and the fluorescence lifetime was reduced by 36 %, which also proved the existence of dynamic quenching mechanism. (Liu et al., 2022; Song et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2021c). In conclusion, the fluorescence quenching caused by NAs on N-CQDs may result from the dynamic quenching caused by NAs colliding with N-CQDs during molecular motion (Wang et al., 2016; Yang et al., 2021c).

The possible synthesis pathways of NADESs and N-CQDs and the possible mechanism of NAs detection by N-CQDs were deduced in Fig. 12 . 5-Amino-tetrazole-monohydrate and polyethylene glycol 400 are hydrogen-bonded to form NADESs, a process in which no chemical reaction occurs. In the hydrothermal process, the interior structure of NADESs was carbonized to form the carbon nuclei of N-CQDs. The surface of N-CQDs is distributed richly in nitrogen-containing groups (NH2) and oxygen-containing groups (OH). After adding NAs to N-CQDs, NAs carries out molecular motion in solution and collides with N-CQDs, leading to dynamic quenching.

Novel nitrogen-rich carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) based on 5-Amino-tetrazole-monohydrate (5-ATZ)/polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG400) NADESs was synthesized by a solvothermal method. N-CQDs is spherical and uniformly distributed, with an average size of 37.8 nm and a nitrogen content of 13.7 %, emitting a bright blue fluorescence. The synthesis mechanism of NADESs and N-CQDs was inferred by FT-IR spectroscopy. The optimal excitation wavelength of N-CQDs is 355 nm, with good temperature, storage time, pH fluorescence stability, and selectivity for NAs. The fluorescence quantum yield of N-CQDs was 7.22 %, and it would be improved by further optimization of composition and structure of precursor.

The fluorescence detection was introduced into quantitative detection of NAs in the petrochemical wastewater. In the model NAs detection, the results showed that the dynamic quenching reaction between N-CQDs and NAs resulted in different degrees of fluorescence quenching, and N-CQDs had good specificity for detecting NAs. N-CQDs was further applied to the wastewater to calculate the concentration of NAs in the wastewater according to the fitted equation. The experiments also used three-dimensional fluorescence spectra to further determine the changes of fluorescence centers and fluorescence intensity after the interaction of N-CQDs with wastewater. Finally, the possible synthesis pathways of NADESs and N-CQDs and the possible mechanism of NAs detection by N-CQDs were further deduced.
